persons.name: Deepak
persons.context: Deepak was found unconscious with severe head injuries near Nehru Nagar, Anand Parbat, Delhi, and later succumbed to his injuries. He was the primary victim of the alleged assault by the accused.

persons.name: Ravinder
persons.context: Ravinder is accused of assaulting Deepak during a quarrel at a marriage function, leading to fatal injuries. He pleaded not guilty to charges under Section 302 IPC (Murder).

persons.name: Dinesh Sharma
persons.context: Dinesh Sharma, the victim’s brother, provided a witness statement claiming he saw the accused near the scene. He is an eyewitness to the events leading to the injury.

title: State vs r
caseText:
CASE FILE: State vs Ravinder
Court: Sessions Court, Delhi
FIR No: 525/2016
Police Station: Anand Parbat, Delhi
Date of FIR: 04 December 2016
INCIDENT SUMMARY:
Victim Deepak was found unconscious with severe head injuries near Nehru Nagar, Anand Parbat, Delhi. A PCR call led police to the scene, and the victim was taken to LHMC Hospital. Initial investigation suggests a quarrel between the accused Ravinder and the victim at a marriage function that escalated into physical assault.
ACCUSED:
Ravinder – allegedly involved in assault leading to fatal injuries
IPC Sections Alleged: 307 IPC (Attempt to Murder), later upgraded to 302 IPC (Murder) after victim succumbed
EVIDENCE COLLECTED BY POLICE:
Witness statement of victim’s brother (saw accused near the scene).
Postmortem examination in hospital (cause: blunt force trauma to head).
Clothes seized from accused with blood stains (sent to FSL).
Accused interrogation notes.
Timeline of incident and initial hospital report.
WITNESSES:
Dinesh Sharma (victim’s brother) – eyewitness to events leading to injury
Hospital staff at LHMC – treated victim on arrival
Police officers present at initial scene
CHARGESHEET / COURT ENTRY:
Investigating Officer filed chargesheet under Section 302 IPC (Murder)
Court received the charge sheet and summoned accused Ravinder
Accused pleaded not guilty

transcript.speaker: Court
transcript.content: Court is now in session. The Honorable Judge presiding. This is the matter of: State vs r. All parties are present. Proceedings will begin with Pre-Trial Motions.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content: The court notes that the charge sheet has been filed under Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code, and the accused, Ravinder, has pleaded not guilty. Before proceeding to the framing of charges, the court calls upon both the Prosecution and the Defense to raise any pre-trial motions, including applications for discharge, suppression of evidence, or bail, if not already disposed of. Counsel, you may now present any such motions.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State has no pre-trial motions to raise at this time. The investigation was conducted in strict accordance with the law, all evidence—including the postmortem report confirming death due to blunt force trauma, the blood-stained clothes seized from the accused, and the eyewitness account of the victim’s brother—was lawfully obtained, and the chargesheet has been properly filed under Section 302 IPC. We are prepared to proceed to the framing of charges and the presentation of our case.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the Defense respectfully moves for discharge under Section 227 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, on the grounds that the evidence presented in the charge sheet—particularly the sole eyewitness account of the victim’s brother and the circumstantial blood-stained clothing—fails to establish a prima facie case of murder under Section 302 IPC. There is no direct evidence linking the accused to the fatal assault, and the prosecution’s case relies entirely on speculation and unverified assumptions. Furthermore, we intend to challenge the admissibility of the accused’s interrogation notes, as they were obtained without proper safeguards under Section 172 BNSS, rendering them inadmissible under Section 23 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has carefully considered the Defense’s application for discharge under Section 227 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023. While the evidence at this stage—namely the eyewitness account of the victim’s brother, the postmortem report indicating death by blunt force trauma, and the blood-stained clothing recovered from the accused—may appear circumstantial, it is sufficient to establish a prima facie case warranting trial, as the threshold for discharge is whether the court is of the opinion that there is *no* evidence to support the charges.  

Regarding the challenge to the admissibility of the interrogation notes, the court notes this is a matter of evidence to be determined during trial under Section 23 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, and not appropriate for pre-trial resolution.  

**Order!** The application for discharge is hereby **rejected**. The court will now proceed to the framing of charges under Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code. The Prosecution may begin its opening statement.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State firmly opposes the defense’s motion to suppress the accused’s interrogation notes, as they were recorded in compliance with Section 172 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, with due regard for the accused’s rights, and contain critical admissions that directly corroborate the eyewitness account and physical evidence. The blood-stained clothing seized from the accused, the postmortem report confirming death by blunt force trauma, and the testimony of the victim’s brother—who witnessed the accused at the scene immediately after the assault—form a coherent and compelling chain of evidence pointing unequivocally to the accused’s guilt. We submit that this is not a case of mere speculation, but one grounded in tangible proof, and we are prepared to demonstrate beyond reasonable doubt that Ravinder is guilty of murder under Section 302 IPC.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, while the Prosecution urges the court to see a coherent chain of evidence, the reality is that this case rests on a single, uncorroborated eyewitness account from the victim’s own brother—a witness with every reason to be biased—and a blood-stained garment whose chain of custody remains unverified and scientifically inconclusive. There is no forensic report confirming the blood belongs to the victim, no murder weapon recovered, and no independent witness placing the accused at the precise moment of the assault. Under the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, circumstantial evidence must form an unbroken chain leading only to guilt, and here, that chain is not only broken—it is built on sand.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has reviewed the charge sheet, the evidence on record, and the arguments presented by both sides. While the Defense contends that the evidence is insufficient and speculative, this court finds that the combination of the eyewitness testimony, the postmortem report, and the recovery of blood-stained clothing from the accused is sufficient to establish a prima facie case under Section 302 IPC.  

**Order!** The court hereby frames the charge of murder under Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code against the accused, Ravinder. The trial shall now proceed with the Prosecution presenting its evidence first. The Prosecution may call its first witness.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State calls Dinesh Sharma, the victim’s brother, to the stand. This witness was present at the marriage function on the night of December 3rd, 2016, and will testify that he saw the accused, Ravinder, in a heated altercation with the deceased, Deepak, moments before Deepak was found unconscious with fatal head injuries. His testimony, combined with the forensic evidence and the blood-stained clothes recovered from the accused, will demonstrate beyond reasonable doubt that Ravinder committed the brutal assault that led to Deepak’s death.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the prosecution’s reliance on the testimony of Dinesh Sharma—the victim’s own brother—is fundamentally compromised by inherent bias and emotional involvement, rendering his account unreliable as the sole basis for a murder conviction. Furthermore, the so-called “blood-stained clothing” lacks conclusive forensic linkage to the victim, and no murder weapon has ever been recovered, leaving a gaping hole in the prosecution’s narrative. Under the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, circumstantial evidence must exclude all other possibilities, yet here, multiple scenarios—including self-defense, an accidental fall, or even third-party involvement—remain entirely plausible and unexamined.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court acknowledges the gravity of the charges and the seriousness with which both sides have presented their positions. The Prosecution has established a prima facie case through the eyewitness account of Dinesh Sharma, corroborated by the postmortem findings of death due to blunt force trauma and the recovery of blood-stained clothing from the accused.  

However, the Defense rightly emphasizes that the burden remains on the State to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt, particularly in light of the lack of a recovered weapon and the need for conclusive forensic linkage of the blood evidence.  

**Order!** The trial shall proceed. The Prosecution may now begin examination-in-chief of Dinesh Sharma.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State submits that the evidence we will present—starting with the eyewitness testimony of Dinesh Sharma, who saw the accused in a violent altercation with his brother moments before the fatal assault—combined with the postmortem report confirming death due to blunt force trauma and the blood-stained clothing seized directly from Ravinder, forms a clear and unbroken chain pointing only to the accused’s guilt. This is not speculation, but a coherent narrative grounded in fact, and we are confident the court will find the prosecution has proven its case beyond reasonable doubt.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the prosecution’s entire case hinges on the testimony of a grieving brother whose account is inherently suspect due to emotional bias, and on blood-stained clothes that have not been conclusively linked through DNA or serology to either the victim or the alleged assault. Without a recovered weapon, without independent corroboration, and without forensic confirmation that the blood is even that of the deceased, the prosecution asks this court to convict based on speculation—not proof. Under the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, mere suspicion, however strong, cannot substitute for evidence that excludes every reasonable hypothesis of innocence.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has heard the arguments from both the Prosecution and the Defense regarding the sufficiency of evidence and the admissibility of materials on record. While the Defense emphasizes the absence of a recovered weapon and questions the forensic linkage of the blood-stained clothing, the court notes that the threshold at this stage is not proof beyond reasonable doubt, but whether a prima facie case exists for the accused to answer.  

**Order!** The charge under Section 302 IPC stands framed. The trial will proceed with the examination of witnesses. The Prosecution may now call Dinesh Sharma to the stand for examination-in-chief.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State firmly submits that the evidence before this court—starting with the eyewitness account of Dinesh Sharma, who saw the accused in a violent confrontation with his brother moments before the assault—coupled with the postmortem report confirming death due to blunt force trauma and the blood-stained clothing recovered directly from Ravinder, forms a clear and unbroken chain of circumstantial evidence pointing only to the accused’s guilt under Section 302 IPC. This is not mere speculation, but a coherent and compelling narrative grounded in tangible proof, and we are confident that the prosecution will demonstrate beyond reasonable doubt that Ravinder committed the brutal murder of Deepak.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the prosecution’s entire case rests on the testimony of a grieving brother whose account is inherently compromised by bias and emotional involvement, and on blood-stained clothing that has not been forensically linked to the victim or the alleged assault. Without a recovered weapon, without independent corroboration, and without conclusive DNA or serological evidence tying the accused to the fatal injuries, the prosecution asks this court to convict based on speculation—not proof beyond reasonable doubt. Under the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, mere suspicion, however strong, cannot substitute for evidence that excludes every reasonable hypothesis of innocence, and here, multiple alternative explanations—including self-defense or third-party involvement—remain entirely plausible and unexamined.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has carefully considered the arguments presented by both the Prosecution and the Defense regarding the framing of charges under Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code. While the Defense raises valid concerns regarding forensic linkage and potential bias in the eyewitness account, the court finds that the combination of the victim’s brother’s testimony, the postmortem report confirming death by blunt force trauma, and the recovery of blood-stained clothing from the accused is sufficient to establish a prima facie case warranting trial.  

**Order!** The charge stands framed. The trial shall now proceed with the Prosecution calling its first witness, Dinesh Sharma, to the stand for examination-in-chief. Counsel, you may begin.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State firmly submits that the evidence before this court—starting with the eyewitness account of Dinesh Sharma, who saw the accused in a violent confrontation with his brother moments before the assault—coupled with the postmortem report confirming death due to blunt force trauma and the blood-stained clothing recovered directly from Ravinder, forms a clear and unbroken chain of circumstantial evidence pointing only to the accused’s guilt under Section 302 IPC. This is not mere speculation, but a coherent and compelling narrative grounded in tangible proof, and we are confident that the prosecution will demonstrate beyond reasonable doubt that Ravinder committed the brutal murder of Deepak.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the prosecution’s case collapses under the weight of its own speculation—relying solely on the testimony of a grieving brother whose account is inherently biased, and on blood-stained clothing that has never been forensically linked to the victim or the alleged assault. Without a recovered weapon, without independent corroboration, and without conclusive DNA or serological evidence, the State asks this court to convict based on suspicion, not proof beyond reasonable doubt. Under the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, mere possibility cannot sustain a murder charge when alternative explanations—such as self-defense, an accidental fall, or third-party involvement—remain entirely plausible and unexamined.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has heard the arguments and reviewed the charge sheet, including the eyewitness account of Dinesh Sharma, the postmortem report confirming death due to blunt force trauma, and the blood-stained clothing recovered from the accused. While the Defense raises concerns regarding forensic linkage and potential bias, the evidence, taken as a whole, is sufficient to establish a prima facie case under Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code.  

**Order!** The charge stands framed. The trial will now proceed with the examination of the prosecution’s first witness. The court calls Dinesh Sharma to the stand. Counsel for the prosecution may begin examination-in-chief.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State will demonstrate that Ravinder brutally assaulted Deepak during a heated altercation at the marriage function, striking him with such force that it caused fatal head injuries consistent with blunt force trauma confirmed in the postmortem report. The eyewitness testimony of Dinesh Sharma—who saw the accused at the scene immediately after the assault—coupled with the blood-stained clothing seized directly from Ravinder, forms an unbroken chain of evidence pointing only to his guilt. We are confident that the totality of this proof will satisfy the court beyond reasonable doubt that the accused is guilty of murder under Section 302 IPC.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the prosecution’s case is built on a foundation of speculation, not proof—relying solely on the testimony of a grieving brother whose account is inherently biased and emotionally compromised, and on blood-stained clothing that has never been forensically linked to the victim or the alleged assault. Without a recovered weapon, without independent corroboration, and without conclusive DNA evidence, the State asks this court to convict my client based on suspicion, not beyond reasonable doubt. Under the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, mere possibility cannot sustain a murder charge when alternative explanations—including self-defense, an accidental fall, or third-party involvement—remain entirely plausible and unexamined.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has carefully considered the arguments presented by both the Prosecution and the Defense regarding the sufficiency of evidence at this stage. While the Defense emphasizes the absence of a recovered weapon and questions the forensic linkage of the blood-stained clothing, the court finds that the eyewitness testimony of Dinesh Sharma, combined with the postmortem report confirming death due to blunt force trauma and the recovery of blood-stained garments from the accused, is sufficient to establish a prima facie case under Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code.  

**Order!** The charge stands framed. The trial shall now proceed with the examination of the prosecution’s first witness. The court calls Dinesh Sharma to the stand. Counsel for the prosecution may begin examination-in-chief.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State calls Dinesh Sharma to the stand—the victim’s brother, who was present at the marriage function on the night of December 3rd, 2016, and witnessed the accused, Ravinder, in a violent altercation with Deepak moments before he was found unconscious with fatal head injuries. His testimony, combined with the postmortem report confirming death due to blunt force trauma and the blood-stained clothing seized directly from the accused, will demonstrate beyond reasonable doubt that Ravinder committed the brutal assault that led to Deepak’s death. We are prepared to present a clear, coherent chain of evidence that leaves no room for reasonable doubt.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the prosecution’s entire case rests on the testimony of a grieving brother whose account is inherently compromised by emotional bias, and on blood-stained clothing that has never been forensically linked—through DNA or serology—to the victim or the alleged assault. Without a recovered weapon, without independent corroboration, and without conclusive scientific evidence tying my client to the fatal injuries, the State asks this court to convict based on suspicion, not proof beyond reasonable doubt. Under the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, mere possibility cannot sustain a murder charge when alternative explanations—including self-defense, an accidental fall, or third-party involvement—remain entirely plausible and unexamined.
